beats.prompt: Quick warm-up — match each quantity to how it behaves.

feedback.correct: A probability is a split, not a flip-count — and Bayes adds: that split can be re-weighed by evidence.
hints: Warm-up from Penney's / Gambler's Ruin — no pressure.
One quantity counts flips (+1 each); the other only splits the chance.
Win-chance → a split (no +1); wait-time → +1 every flip.

beats.prompt: I have two coins — one fair, one with heads on both sides. I pick one at random, flip it, and get heads. Is it more likely the two-headed coin?

options: Still 50/50 — a head can't rule out either coin
It's certain — it must be the two-headed coin

Still 50/50 — a head can't rule out eith.note: Both coins CAN show heads — but not equally often. The two-headed coin shows heads every time; the fair coin only half. Watch what 'how often' does.

More likely the two-headed coin.note: Good instinct — a head is better explained by the two-headed coin. Let's find exactly how much.

It's certain — it must be the two-headed.note: Strong — but a fair coin shows heads too (half the time). Evidence tilts the split; it rarely settles it.

hints: No wrong guess yet — commit and we'll weigh it.
A head is twice as likely from the two-headed coin.
It moves you 1/2 → 2/3 — not to certainty.

beats.prompt: Read this just-in-time primer on prior, likelihood, and posterior — then continue.

interaction.title: Prior, likelihood, posterior
interaction.body: Your belief before the flip is the prior. How well each coin predicts the flip you saw is the likelihood. Your updated belief after is the posterior. Bayes says: posterior ∝ prior × likelihood.

feedback.correct: Prior → likelihood → posterior. Keep those three words.
hints: Before, during, after.
Likelihood = how well a hypothesis predicted what you saw.
posterior ∝ prior × likelihood.

beats.prompt: Drag the prior split, pick the evidence (heads), and watch the posterior bar swing.

hero.structuralReadout: A head leaves 2 of 3 equally-likely heads on the two-headed coin → 2/3.

feedback.correct: Drag the prior and the posterior bar swings — one head moves 1/2 → 2/3, never to certainty.
hints: Drag the prior split, then pick the evidence (heads).
Evidence tilts the split; it doesn't prove or erase a coin.
At a 1/2 prior, a single head lands you at 2/3.

beats.prompt: Of the 3 equally-likely heads, tap the ones that sit on the two-headed coin.

feedback.correct: Two of the three equally-likely heads sit on the two-headed coin → 2/3.
hints: The two-headed coin offers two heads (one per face); the fair coin offers one.
Of the 3 equally-likely heads, tap the 2 that belong to the two-headed coin — the two H-sides are NOT 'the same' head.
2 of 3 heads are the two-headed coin's → P = 2/3.

beats.prompt: Compute P(two-headed | heads) using prior × likelihood ÷ total. Give your answer as a fraction.

feedback.correct: (½·1)/(½·1 + ½·½) = (½)/(¾) = 2/3. The 100% likelihood is weighed by the prior and renormalized.
hints: Don't stop at the likelihood (1). Weigh it by the prior, then divide by the total chance of a head.
Numerator ½·1; denominator ½·1 + ½·½ = ¾ (posterior = likelihood is the trap).

beats.prompt: A family has two children. Compute P(both boys) under each clue.

fields.label: P(both boys | at least one is a boy)
P(both boys | this child is a boy)

feedback.correct: 'At least one is a boy' removes only girl-girl → 1/3. 'This specific child is a boy' is stronger evidence → 1/2. The framing of the clue changes the update.
hints: Same family, two different clues — list {bb, bg, gb, gg} and remove what each clue rules out.
'At least one boy' keeps {bb, bg, gb} → 1 of 3. 'This child is a boy' points at one child → 1 of 2.
At-least-one → 1/3; this-child → 1/2.

beats.prompt: Three methods all converge on 2/3 — reveal each lens.

lenses.label: Frequencies
lenses.body: 3 equally-likely heads, 2 on the two-headed coin

beats.interviewNote: The two-coin update is the cleanest Bayes warm-up: posterior odds = prior odds × likelihood ratio (1:1 × 2:1 = 2:1 → 2/3). Naming the likelihood ratio out loud is the answer.

feedback.correct: Formula, frequencies, and odds all land on 2/3 — not a trick of one method.
hints: Reveal each lens.
They are three roads to the same number.
All three say 2/3.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: same two coins — you flip the chosen coin and see heads twice (HH). Give the exact posterior.

interaction.scenario: Same two coins (one fair, one two-headed). You flip the chosen coin twice and see \(HH\).

feedback.correct: Apply the posterior formula: \[ P(H\mid E) = \frac{P(H)\,P(E\mid H)}{\sum_j P(H_j)\,P(E\mid H_j)} \] Priors: \(P(\text{TH}) = P(\text{fair}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\). Two-head likelihoods: \(P(HH\mid\text{TH}) = 1\) and \(P(HH\mid\text{fair}) = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}\). Substituting: \[ P(\text{TH}\mid HH) = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 1}{\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\tfrac{1}{4}} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}}{\tfrac{5}{8}} = \frac{4}{5} \] The two-headed coin is four times as likely to show \(HH\) as the fair coin — a second head pushes the posterior from \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) to \(\tfrac{4}{5}\).
hints: A second head multiplies the evidence again — the fair coin's two-head likelihood is now \(\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}\), not \(\tfrac{1}{2}\).
Numerator: \(\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 1 = \tfrac{1}{2}\); denominator: \(\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\tfrac{1}{4} = \tfrac{5}{8}\).

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap to review the core principle.

feedback.correct: Evidence rescales belief in proportion to how well each hypothesis predicted it: posterior ∝ prior × likelihood, then renormalize.
hints: A head moves 1/2 → 2/3 because 2 of 3 equally-likely heads are the two-headed coin's.
The prior never drops out — you weigh the likelihood by it and divide by the total.
Next up: when the prior is tiny, even strong evidence barely moves it.
